question: The escape velocity of a body depends upon mass as

q: Which graph correctly presents the variation of acceleration due to gravity with the distance from the centre of the earth (radius of the earth $=R_E$ )?
question: Which graph correctly presents the variation of acceleration due to gravity with the distance from the centre of the earth (radius of the earth $=R_E$ )?
solution: Acceleration due to gravity, $g \propto r$ (if $r < R_E$ ) and $g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}\left(\right.$ if $\left.r>R_E\right)$

q: Two bodies of masses $\mathrm{m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ are placed at a distance $\mathrm{r}$. The gravitational potential at a point on the line joining them where the gravitational field is zero is:
question: Two bodies of masses $\mathrm{m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ are placed at a distance $\mathrm{r}$. The gravitational potential at a point on the line joining them where the gravitational field is zero is:
solution:
Position of the null point from mass $\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{r}}{1+\sqrt{\frac{4 \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{~m}}}}=\frac{r}{3}$
$$
V=-G m\left(\frac{3}{r}+\frac{12}{2 r}\right)=-9 \frac{G m}{r}
$$

q: At a certain depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below surface of earth, value of acceleration due to gravity becomes four times that of its value at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> above earth surface. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is Radius of earth (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>). The depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is equal to
question: At a certain depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below surface of earth, value of acceleration due to gravity becomes four times that of its value at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> above earth surface. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is Radius of earth (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>). The depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
The magnitude of acceleration due to gravity g1 at a depth of d below the surface of Earth is

g1=GMR3R-d=GMR21-dR    ....(1)

The magnitude of acceleration due to gravity g2 at a height d above the surface of Earth is

g2=GMR+d2    ....(2)

Given here, g1=4g2

GMR21-dR=4GMR+3R2

⇒GMR21-dR=4GM4R2

⇒1-dR=14⇒dR=34⇒d=34R

⇒d=4800 km

subject: Physics
q: If a body of mass $1$ kg falls on the earth from infinity, it attains velocity $(v)$ and kinetic energy $(k)$ on reaching the surface of earth. The values of $v$ and $k$ respectively are _______. (Take radius of earth to be $6400$ km and $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$)
question: If a body of mass $1$ kg falls on the earth from infinity, it attains velocity $(v)$ and kinetic energy $(k)$ on reaching the surface of earth. The values of $v$ and $k$ respectively are _______. (Take radius of earth to be $6400$ km and $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
By conservation of mechanical energy, the total energy of the body at infinity is zero.

When the body reaches the surface of the earth, its potential energy is $-\dfrac{GMm}{R}$ and kinetic energy is $k = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.

$0 = k - \dfrac{GMm}{R}$

$k = \dfrac{GMm}{R} = mgR$

Substituting $m = 1$ kg, $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$, and $R = 6400 \times 10^3$ m:

$k = 1 \times 9.8 \times 6400 \times 10^3 = 6.272 \times 10^7$ J

The velocity $v$ is given by $\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = k$.

$v = \sqrt{2gR} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 6400 \times 10^3}$

$v = \sqrt{125.44 \times 10^6} = 11.2 \times 10^3$ m/s $= 11.2$ km/s

Answer: $11.2$ km/s; $6.27 \times 10^7$ J

q:
If suddenly the gravitational force of attraction between Earth and a satellite revolving around it
becomes zero, then the satellite will
question:
If suddenly the gravitational force of attraction between Earth and a satellite revolving around it
becomes zero, then the satellite will
options: continue to move in its orbit with same velocity
move tangentially to the originally orbit in the same velocity
solution: When gravitational force becomes zero so centripetal force on satellite becomes zero so satellite will escape its round orbit and becomes stationary.

q: Four identical particles of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> are kept at the four corners of a square. If the gravitational force exerted on one of the masses by the other masses is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>32</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math>, the length of the sides of the square is
question: Four identical particles of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> are kept at the four corners of a square. If the gravitational force exerted on one of the masses by the other masses is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>32</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math>, the length of the sides of the square is
solution:
Resultant force, Fnet=2F+F', where

F=Gm2a2 and F'=Gm22a2

Therefore, Fnet=2Gm2a2+Gm22a2

Comparing with the expression given in the question, we get

22+132Gm2L2=Gm2a222+12

⇒a=4L

q:
Assume there are two identical simple pendulum Clocks-1 is placed on the earth and Clock-2 is placed on a space station located at a height h above the earth surface. Clock-1 and Clock-2 operate at time periods 4 s and 6 s respectively. Then the value of h is -
(consider radius of earth RE=6400 km and g on earth 10 m s-2)
question:
Assume there are two identical simple pendulum Clocks-1 is placed on the earth and Clock-2 is placed on a space station located at a height h above the earth surface. Clock-1 and Clock-2 operate at time periods 4 s and 6 s respectively. Then the value of h is -
(consider radius of earth RE=6400 km and g on earth 10 m s-2)
solution:
The time period of a simple pendulum is given by, T=2πlg.

Therefore, T∝1g.

The acceleration due to gravity at height h from the surface of the earth is given by, g'=gR+h2

Therefore,

T1T2=g2g1⇒46=RR+h⇒h=R2=3200 km

solution:
Weight =mGMR2
∴94=GMeRe2×Rp2GMp
Given that, Mp=Me9
∴94=MeMe9×Rp2Re2
Rp2=Re2×14
Rp=Re2

subject: Physics
q: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, a body weighed $300 \mathrm{~N}$ on the surface of earth. How much it would weigh at R/4 depth under surface of earth ?
question: Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, a body weighed $300 \mathrm{~N}$ on the surface of earth. How much it would weigh at R/4 depth under surface of earth ?
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { At surface: } \mathrm{mg}=300 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \mathrm{~m}=\frac{300}{\mathrm{~g}_{\mathrm{s}}} \\ & \text { At Depth } \frac{\mathrm{R}}{4}: \mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{d}}=\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{s}}\left[1-\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{R}}\right] \\ & \mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{d}}=\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{s}}\left[1-\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4 \mathrm{R}}\right] \\ & \mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~g}_{\mathrm{s}}}{4}\end{aligned}$
weight at depth $\quad=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{d}}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{m} \times \frac{3 \mathrm{~g}_{\mathrm{s}}}{4} \\ & =\frac{3}{4} \times 300 \\ & =225 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

As we know, escape velocity is given by, vescape=2GMR

Now, vplanet=2GMR=v

vMoon=2GM×16144×R=132GMR=v3

subject: Physics
q:
A planet revolving in elliptical orbit has :

A. a constant velocity of revolution.

B. has the least velocity when it is nearest to the sun.

C. its areal velocity is directly proportional to its velocity.

D. areal velocity is inversely proportional to its velocity.

E. to follow a trajectory such that the areal velocity is constant.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
A planet revolving in elliptical orbit has :

A. a constant velocity of revolution.

B. has the least velocity when it is nearest to the sun.

C. its areal velocity is directly proportional to its velocity.

D. areal velocity is inversely proportional to its velocity.

E. to follow a trajectory such that the areal velocity is constant.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution: As per Kepler’s second law “The areal velocity of a planet revolving around the sun in elliptical orbit remains constant which implies the angular momentum of a planet remains constant”. Hence (option E) is the correct option.

q:
The approximate height from the surface of earth at which the weight of the body becomes 13 of its weight on the surface of earth is :
[Radius of earth R=6400 km and 3=1.732]
question:
The approximate height from the surface of earth at which the weight of the body becomes 13 of its weight on the surface of earth is :
[Radius of earth R=6400 km and 3=1.732]
solution:
The variation of the acceleration due to gravity with height h is given by,

g'=g1+hR2.

Since the weight at given height is 13 of the weight at the surface

⇒g'=g3⇒g1+hR2=g3⇒1+hR=3⇒h=R3-1=6400×1.732-1=4685 km

subject: Physics
q: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> move round the earth in the same orbit. The mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is twice the mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. The quantity which is same for the two satellites will be
question: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> move round the earth in the same orbit. The mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is twice the mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. The quantity which is same for the two satellites will be
solution:
The formula to calculate the speed of a satellite while revolving around the Earth is given by

v=GMeR   ...1

where, v is the speed, G is the Gravitational constant, Me is the mass of the Earth, R is the radius of the orbit.

As can be seen from the equation, speed is independent of mass of satellite.

Hence, the speed of the satellites will be the same.

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is revolving in a circular orbit at a height h from the carth surface, such that $\mathrm{h}< < \mathrm{R}$ where $\mathrm{R}$ is the radius of the earth. Assuming that the effect of earth"s atmosphere can be neglected the minimum increase in the speed required so that the satellite could escape from the gravitational field of earth is
question: A satellite is revolving in a circular orbit at a height h from the carth surface, such that $\mathrm{h}< < \mathrm{R}$ where $\mathrm{R}$ is the radius of the earth. Assuming that the effect of earth"s atmosphere can be neglected the minimum increase in the speed required so that the satellite could escape from the gravitational field of earth is
solution:
For a satellite orbiting close to the earth, orbital velocity is given by

$v_{0}=\sqrt{g(R+h)}=\sqrt{g R}$

Escape velocity $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)$ is

$v_{e}=\sqrt{2 g(R+h)}=\sqrt{2 g R} \quad[\because h  < c R$

$\Delta v=v_{e}-v_{0}=(\sqrt{2}-1) \sqrt{g R}$

The subsequent motion of the combined body will be:
question: A satellite of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> about the center of the earth. A meteorite of the same mass, falling towards the earth, collides with the satellite completely inelastic. The speeds of the satellite and the meteorite are the same, just before the collision. The subsequent motion of the combined body will be:
options: Such that it escapes to infinity
In a circular orbit of a different radius
In the same circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math>
solution:
2mvx=mv

vx=v2

Similarly vy=v2

Vnet=v22+v22=v2
As resultant velocity after collision is less than orbital velocity and it's direction is neither radial nor tangential , the combined mass will follow elliptical orbit

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is revolving in a circular orbit at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> from the earth's surface (radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>; <math> <mrow> <mtext>h</mtext> <mtext><<</mtext> <mtext>R</mtext> </mrow> </math> ). The minimum increase in its orbital velocity required, so that the satellite could escape from the earth's gravitational field, is close to (Neglect the effect of atmosphere.)
question: A satellite is revolving in a circular orbit at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> from the earth's surface (radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>; <math> <mrow> <mtext>h</mtext> <mtext><<</mtext> <mtext>R</mtext> </mrow> </math> ). The minimum increase in its orbital velocity required, so that the satellite could escape from the earth's gravitational field, is close to (Neglect the effect of atmosphere.)
solution:
Since,  h << R ,

Ve=2gR

& V0=gR

∴The minimum increase in velocity required,

Ve-V0=2gR-gR

=2-1gR

subject: Physics
q: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
question: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at height h is given by, g'=g1+hR2

For distance from the centre r=54R height h=R4,

⇒g'=g1+142=1625g

Change in weight 

∆W=mg-mg'⇒∆W=mg1-1625

⇒∆W=925mg

Percentage change =925×100%

=36%

subject: Physics
q: The time period of an earth satellite in circular orbit is independent of
question: The time period of an earth satellite in circular orbit is independent of
options: both the mass and radius of the orbit
neither the mass of the satellite nor the radius of its orbit.
solution:
The time period of satellite is given by
$$
T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h})^3}{\mathrm{GM}}}
$$
where, $\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{h}=$ radius of orbit satellite, $\mathrm{M}=$ mass of earth.

subject: Physics
q: Spherical insulating ball and a spherical metallic ball of same size and mass are dropped from the same height. Choose the correct statement out of the following {Assume negligible air friction}
question: Spherical insulating ball and a spherical metallic ball of same size and mass are dropped from the same height. Choose the correct statement out of the following {Assume negligible air friction}
options: Time taken by them to reach the earth's surface will be independent of the properties of their materials
Insulating ball will reach the earth's surface earlier than the metal ball
Both will reach the earth's surface simultaneously
Metal ball will reach the earth's surface earlier than the insulating ball
solution: When a spherical insulating ball and a spherical metallic ball of same size and mass are dropped from the same height, then magnetic field will act on metallic ball. If the earth's magnetic field is taken into account, then an induced eddy current will form inside the spherical metallic ball, thus retarding its motion. Hence, it will take more time than insulating ball.

subject: Physics
q: A very long (length L) cylindrical galaxy is made of uniformly distributed mass and has radius R (R<<L). A star outside the galaxy is orbiting the galaxy in a plane perpendicular to the galaxy and passing through its centre. If the time period of the star is T and its distance from the galaxy's axis is r, then
question: A very long (length L) cylindrical galaxy is made of uniformly distributed mass and has radius R (R<<L). A star outside the galaxy is orbiting the galaxy in a plane perpendicular to the galaxy and passing through its centre. If the time period of the star is T and its distance from the galaxy's axis is r, then
solution:
Due to a long solid cylinder gravitational field strength can be given as,

g= 2 G λr

Where,

λ= linear mass density of galaxy.  

For the orbital motion around the galaxy,



fg=fcentripetal

⇒       mg=m ω2r 

⇒       2Gλr=ω2r

⇒      ω2 ∝1r2

⇒      ω ∝1r

⇒      2πT ∝1r     ⇒     T∝r

So option B is correct.
